Speaker 4: Turning now to our three action items for today.
Speaker 4: Item number six is proposed ordinance 20260108.
Speaker 4: which relates to the sale of surplus property in Townsville District 9.
Speaker 4: Are we going to have a briefing on this one?
Speaker 4: April, who will do it?
Speaker 7: I will.
Speaker 4: You are going to do it, okay.
Speaker 7: Good morning.
Speaker 7: April Sanders, Council Policy Staff.
Speaker 7: I am pinch-hitting for Brandi Parabello today.
Speaker 7: The proposed ordinance would approve the sale of real property that has been declared surplus.
Speaker 7: The property was previously under the custodianship of King County Water and Land Resources Division, or Willard.
Speaker 7: King County received the property from Washington State Department of Natural Resources in 2001 as part of the trust land transfer, and the property consists of 39.1 acres of agricultural land located south of the city of Enumclaw in Council District 9.
Speaker 7: Willard determined it was surplus to their needs and F M D conducted the surplus declaration analysis, resulting in the property being declared surplus on September 8th, 2021.

Speaker 7: The county subsequently advertised the property on M L S and entered into a purchase and sale agreement, which is attachment eight to the proposed ordinance.
Speaker 7: The appraisal utilized a sales comparison approach among five land sales to reach an appraisal amount of six hundred,zero, which is the purchase price.
Speaker 7: And the purchase and sale agreement includes a bargain and sale deed that is subject to a reserved agricultural easement upon closing of the sale.
Speaker 7: The council approval period as extended expires on August 16th, 2026 and proceeds from the sale would provide one-time revenues to Willard for the acquisition of new open space property and improve recreation facilities at existing sites.
Speaker 7: The declaration of surplus and the proposed sale of the property appears consistent with county code requirements.
Speaker 7: There is a single amendment, Amendment one, which would add Attachment B, which would extend the council approval period from the original June 17th date to August 17th, 2026.
Speaker 7: And Amanda Tran is here from F M D to answer questions.
Speaker 4: Okay, thank you very much.
Speaker 4: Do members have questions for our staff or executive staff?
Speaker 4: Did you mention there was an easement that will run with the property for at least some period of time?

Speaker 4: Did you mention there was an easement that will run with the property for at least some period of time?

Speaker 4: question.
Speaker 4: I was just curious.
Speaker 4: It seemed to me it was term limited to maybe thirty years, and I was wondering why.
Speaker 10: Hi, Amanda Tran from F M D, real property agent on this sale.
Speaker 10: The thirty-year requirement is because this property was received from the state D N R, and one of the conditions of receiving this property is to preserve it as agricultural land for that period of time.
Speaker 10: So the restrictive covenant for this property enforces that,
Speaker 10: and it needs to enforce that.

Speaker 10: perpetuity?

Speaker 10: Okay.

Speaker 9: And so I am wondering if this is one of those examples where they are

Speaker 4: Turning now to item seven, which relates, is item, or is proposed ordinance 2026-sixty nine, relating to the sale of

Speaker 4: Turning now to item seven, which relates, is item, or is proposed ordinance 2026-sixty nine, relating to the sale of
Speaker 4: property in Kent, Washington Council District 5.
Speaker 4: Nick Bowman is joining us to give us an overview of this item.
Speaker 1: Good morning.
Speaker 1: For the record, Nick Bowman, Council Central staff.
Speaker 1: The materials for agenda item number seven begin on page one hundred thirteen of your packet.
Speaker 1: Proposed ordinance 2026-sixty nine would approve the sale of nearly one hundred eighty,zero square feet of county-owned property located in the city of Kent for approximately one hundred fourteen,000.
Speaker 1: Back in 1990, King County acquired Clark Lake Park with state grant funding that required the property to be preserved permanently for outdoor recreation.
Speaker 1: The city of Kent then expanded its boundary to include the park in January of 1996.
Speaker 1: In May of 1996, Kent and King County entered into an I L A to widen Southeast 240th Street, which is a major arterial running along the northern boundary line of the park.
Speaker 1: As part of the project, a portion of the park needed to be converted into a stormwater drainage pond.
Speaker 1: Under the terms of the 1996 I L A, the county would purchase replacement park land to be transferred to the city after the city paid for half of the property acquisition costs.

Speaker 1: However, the city never paid its half and the county never transferred the property.
Speaker 1: The park has been used exclusively as park and open space since it was purchased and roads, which originally purchased the property, has no use for it.
Speaker 1: Maps of the property can be found on page one hundred fifteen and one hundred sixteen of the packet.
Speaker 1: There are a couple issues with the proposed ordinance, but the primary policy issue is the proposed purchase price of one hundred fourteen,zero, which is based off the parcel's 1997 property value.
Speaker 1: King County Code four hundred fifty six-one hundred exempts sales to governmental agencies from the requirement that property be sold to the highest responsible bidder at public auction or by sealed bid.
Speaker 1: and 456.140 allowed the sale of county property to government agencies upon such terms as agreed upon and deemed adequate by the county.
Speaker 1: According to the executive, the county and the city have agreed to use the 1997 property value because that is what was originally intended under the I L A and what was approved by the state recreation agency which provided the initial grant funding.
Speaker 1: Another issue is an incomplete purchase and sale agreement, which was transmitted with the proposed ordinance.

Speaker 1: Another issue is an incomplete purchase and sale agreement, which was transmitted with the proposed ordinance.
Speaker 1: The transmitted purchase and sale agreement, which is attachment A to the ordinance, did not include a signed First Amendment that extends the Council approval period until July twenty four, 2026, and also includes a restrictive covenant intended to ensure the property must always be used as a city park and open space.
Speaker 1: Without this amendment to the P S A, the Council approval period would have already lapsed, in effect voiding the sale.
Speaker 1: There is an amendment to correct this error, which I will brief in just a moment.
Speaker 1: The last issue is a matter of timing.
Speaker 1: With the first amendment to the P S A, the proposed ordinance must be effective by July twenty four, 2026.
Speaker 1: To meet this deadline, this committee would need to approve the ordinance today and expedite the proposed ordinance to the full council for approval on July 14th.
Speaker 1: The full council would then need to approve the ordinance to become effective by the July 24th deadline.
Speaker 1: So we are running up there against right up to it.
Speaker 1: That concludes my remarks, but I will quickly brief the amendment.
Speaker 1: Amendment one on page one hundred forty five would delete the transmitted attachment A, the purchase and sale agreement, and replace it with a new attachment A, a complete purchase and sale agreement, dated June ten, 2026.

Speaker 1: That includes that signed first amendment.
Speaker 1: And that concludes my remarks.
Speaker 1: Happy to answer any questions.
Speaker 4: Okay, Nick.
Speaker 4: Thank you.
Speaker 4: Council Member Fain is the prime sponsor of this legislation properties in her district.
Speaker 4: Would you like to put it before us and speak to it?
Speaker 4: Council Member Fain.
Speaker 8: Thank you so much.
Speaker 8: Move to approve this motion.
Speaker 8: This is a commitment that was made many, many years ago.
Speaker 8: I do not know all the details around what happened in 1997 and 1998, but this transfers currently.
Speaker 8: It is property that is currently used as parks and open space.
Speaker 8: It is adjacent to Clark Lake Park, which is a huge city of Kent.
Speaker 8: maintain park.
Speaker 8: an open space, and it was intended to rain that way for Kent residents, but the transfer was never completed.
Speaker 8: So this allows both parties to fulfill that original agreement, preserves the intended use through a restrictive covenant, and kind of resolves that longstanding, outstanding obligation between the county and the city.
Speaker 8: And is there support?
Speaker 4: All right, thank you so much.
Speaker 4: Would you like to offer the amendment?
Speaker 4: Is it Amendment one, Nick?
Speaker 1: Amendment one, yes.
Speaker 4: Amendment 1.
Speaker 8: Amendment 1.
Speaker 4: Okay, Amendment one makes the changes Nick described.

Speaker 4: Is it Amendment one, Nick?
Speaker 1: Amendment one, yes.
Speaker 4: Amendment 1.
Speaker 8: Amendment 1.
Speaker 4: Okay, Amendment one makes the changes Nick described.
Speaker 4: All in favor, say aye.
Speaker 4: Aye.

Speaker 4: That carries.
Speaker 4: Underlying ordinance as amended.
Speaker 4: Comments?
Speaker 4: So I would just say a little unusual on the purchase price, but in the spirit of accommodation and partnership with our good partner in the city of Kent, and given the uniqueness of this, it seems that it is appropriate to use the exception, and I am happy to support it.
Speaker 4: Council Member Fain, do you want to close?
Speaker 8: I do not think I have anything yet.
Speaker 8: I appreciate your support.

Speaker 4: Okay, by that vote, we have given a unanimous do pass recommendation to proposed ordinance 2026-0069.
Speaker 4: As requested, we will expedite that to the July 14th council meeting and have it on consent.
Speaker 4: All right.
Speaker 4: Seeing nods there.

Speaker 4: Turning to our last action item today, number eight on the agenda, Proposed Ordinance 2026-0083.
Speaker 4: This would allow some additional flexibility in our interim loan program.
Speaker 4: And I think I have got to let staff brief it.
Speaker 4: I will put it that way.
Speaker 4: How about who is going to do this one?
Speaker 4: Oh, you are?
Speaker 4: All right.
Speaker 4: Go ahead.
Speaker 7: April Sanders Council policy staff, the materials for item eight begin on page two hundred eleven of your packet.
Speaker 7: The proposed ordinance would amend the D C H S interim loan program to allow extended loan terms and more flexible area median income requirements.
Speaker 7: Catholic Housing Services, or C H S, holds an 8.1 million interim loan for the Aurora Oaks site and shoreline, which was due in June twenty nine, 2026.
Speaker 7: C H S notified D C H S that it cannot repay the loan or complete permanent supportive housing on schedule due to federal, state, and local funding uncertainties.
Speaker 7: C H S has proposed a redevelopment plan for one hundred units serving households at fifty to sixty A M I, including partial demolition and new construction.
Speaker 7: Moving forward on this amended project would require two changes to the interim loan program, which this proposed ordinance would effectuate.

Speaker 7: First, allowing for an extended loan term of up to an additional five years.
Speaker 7: And second, raising the permissible area median income from fifty to sixty.
Speaker 7: As a bit of background, the interim loan program allows for the use of homelessness housing and services program monies, largely document recording fees, and mental illness and drug dependency housing services monies within the housing and community development fund to offer loans to experienced housing developers on a short-term interim basis in order to acquire property for affordable housing and homeless housing.
Speaker 7: King County Code limits the total loan amount of fifteen million at any given time.
Speaker 7: King County code also states that the interim loan program is intended to add to the stock of housing for low income and special needs residents.
Speaker 7: The program is available again to experienced housing developers to acquire property for affordable and homeless housing for households at or below fifty area median income and loans must be repaid within five years.
Speaker 7: Like I mentioned, King County provided an interim loan of 8.1 million to C H S in June of 2021.
Speaker 7: It is a five-year loan at three simple interest due upon maturity of the loan, and the loan amount plus interest is estimated at 9.3 million.

Speaker 7: The project was to acquire a 2.6-acre site and shoreline at the former Oaks at Forest Bay skilled nursing facility to redevelop as permanent supportive housing or an alternate housing strategy.
Speaker 7: Currently, Lake City Partners Ending Homelessness, or Lake City Partners, operates the sixty-bed Aurora Oaks Enhanced Shelter under an agreement with C H S. Ultimately, C H S intends to redevelop the site as permanent supportive housing, prioritizing those at least twenty five of units for homeless households, and that intent is memorialized in the loan agreement.
Speaker 7: As the maturity date approached, C H S did notify in early 2025 that it could not fulfill the loan obligations.
Speaker 7: C H S cited again that federal volatility, particularly with continuum of care homeless housing funding and a limited state and local money to support homeless housing.
Speaker 7: In the 2026-2027 Biennial Budget Ordinance, Proviso three on the Housing Community Development Fund restricted three hundred,zero until the Executive transmits a report describing the next steps required for the Aurora Oaks Enhanced Shelter to maintain operations as a shelter on site.
Speaker 7: The Executive transmitted the resulting report on April eight, 2026 with this accompanying proposed ordinance.
Speaker 7: The report analyzed three options for the continued operations of the enhanced shelter.

Speaker 7: The report analyzed three options for the continued operations of the enhanced shelter.
Speaker 7: Option one, to amend King County Code to allow for an extension of C H S's loan, which is recommended by the executive.
Speaker 7: Option two, foreclose on the C H S interim loan.
Speaker 7: And option three, purchase the Aurora Oaks site from C H S. The report provides advantages and disadvantages to each, but recommends option one, which would allow the shelter to continue operations onsite until C H S is ready to begin development.
Speaker 7: Moving on to the analysis and key issues on page two hundred sixteen of your packet.
Speaker 7: Key issue A is the proviso focus.
Speaker 7: The intent of proviso P3 was to determine options to allow continued operations of the enhanced shelter in the long term.
Speaker 7: The selected option in the report amending the interim loan program focuses primarily on the C H S loan and how to address the fact that the loan was due at the end of June.
Speaker 7: While this would allow the Aurora Oaks Enhanced Shelter to continue operations for the time being, it is not a permanent solution as the final C H S project would likely preclude the continued use of the property as a shelter, as interim loan program dollars are required to go towards the development of affordable or homeless housing and not shelters.

Speaker 7: Key issue B, project feasibility.
Speaker 7: When asked whether the executive believes the project is financially feasible and whether the extended loan would likely result in a completed project, executive staff stated that it is feasible if permitted to do a partial redevelopment of the site.
Speaker 7: However, this is dependent on the financial forecast of public funding and relative priorities.
Speaker 7: So there is still a risk that extending the loan may not result in a completed project.
Speaker 7: Lastly, Key Issue C. If the proposed ordinance is not adopted and C H S is unable to repay the interim loan, the property would technically fall into default and D C H S would need to determine whether to initiate foreclosure proceedings.
Speaker 7: Executive staff state that it is likely that D C H S and C H S would seek a remedy in lieu of a foreclosure, which would likely result in King County via the Facilities Management Division becoming owners of the property and C H S foregoing development rights.
Speaker 7: There are two amendments included in your packet, which are summarized on page 217.
Speaker 7: First, striking amendment S1 would make technical and clarifying changes to the proposed ordinance.
Speaker 7: And amendment one to S1 would allow the executive to decrease or waive interest charged.

Speaker 7: And amendment one to S1 would allow the executive to decrease or waive interest charged.
Speaker 7: And that brings me to the end of my remarks.
Speaker 7: We have Kristen Pula here to answer any questions of executive staff.
Speaker 4: Okay, thank you very much, April.
Speaker 4: Do members have questions on this?
Speaker 4: Chair, I have one question.

Speaker 4: Council Member Lewis.

Speaker 6: Why are we doing that, and what do we expect the result to be?

Speaker 7: So C H S determined it was financially infeasible to construct a project that was fifty or lower.
Speaker 7: So this is to hopefully accommodate an ending project being built.
Speaker 7: They think it is financially feasible if it is sixty as the threshold instead of 50.

Speaker 3: So even though this is being presented as applying to this particular
Speaker 3: know to facilitate this particular situation we are kind of changing this going forward for future loan applications it would be up to sixty percent um so just trying to figure out if there is any consequences to that in the future but but i guess the there is discretion and we do not have to do it for every project sixty percent there is still discretion i see some folks note in the back so that is that is that is helpful thank you

Speaker 7: Correct, especially if the financial outlook for affordable housing projects shifted, then the executive would have the authority to keep in the contract the fifty, but there would be the allowance to go up to sixty.
Speaker 3: Thank you.
Speaker 4: Thank you.
Speaker 4: This may be a question for exec staff, but I thought we should get on the record.
Speaker 4: With respect to the repayment, because this authorizes an extension, it is my understanding that executive staff
Speaker 4: contemplate that there will be an application for the fall T O D grant program and that this project would, well you cannot guarantee it, but my understanding is that you feel it would compete fairly well and those T O D dollars may provide, this is not my question, is it your expectation that those T O D dollars would provide a funding source for C H S to repay the loan?
Speaker 5: Thank you, Council Members.
Speaker 5: Kristen Pula, I am the Chief of Capital Programs in the Housing and Community Development Division.
Speaker 5: Jelani Jackson, our Acting Director, is in Kirkland all day, so I am standing in for him.
Speaker 5: Thank you so much for the question.
Speaker 5: Yes, we anticipate C H S applying in our fall round for the ten million that is available that you made available in the 2025 budget for T O D projects in Shoreline or Kenmore.

Speaker 5: At this point, I think they will be very competitive for that funding.
Speaker 5: I believe they told us, so they are working on a full application right now.
Speaker 5: The one they submitted last year was kind of a surface level application because they did not know yet if they could go up to sixty A M I.
Speaker 5: So they are working on a real in-depth application right now, getting bids, and we expect to see that in September.
Speaker 5: What they have told us in conversation or what they said last year in the application is they thought they would only be able to repay five of the eight at the initial phase.
Speaker 5: But we do not know if that will change in the next application.
Speaker 5: So I think it depends on whether they are able to pick up other public funding from the state.
Speaker 5: in this funding round, you know, it could take another year for them to secure all the funding.
Speaker 5: But at least from what they submitted last year, we think they will be able to repay five at first, and then the other three would come at a subsequent phase.
Speaker 4: Okay, thank you.
Speaker 4: That is very helpful.

Speaker 4: Okay, thank you.

Speaker 4: Just for additional background, this was a former kind of what we used to call a rest home, a senior care facility that is been used very successfully as a shelter, about sixty two beds run by Lake City Partners.
Speaker 4: My interest, as I have shared before, is in ensuring we do not lose the shelter bed capacity, and I am pleased to say we had a
Speaker 4: what I thought was a very good meeting with C H S a couple of weeks ago.
